content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 6, 2016

Rae Bell Arbogast
President, ACWD
Alleghany County Water District
P.O. Box 860
Alleghany, CA 95910

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-060

Dear Mr. Bell Arbogast:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Also, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTIONS

1. May you take part in a decision of the Alleghany County Water District (the "District") Board to reinstate a proposed water project plan (the "Original Plan") after an alternate plan (the "Current Plan") has been approved where both plans affect property owned by Original Sixteen To One Mine, Inc., (the "Corporation") of which you are the Secretary and a shareholder?

2. May you act as the primary coordinator with engineers working on the project?

3. May you participate in decisions involving the construction phase of the project after the planning phase has been completed?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-060
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. It is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will materially affect your
financial interest.

2. and 3. Yes. You have not identified any governmental decisions that may come before
the District's Board that would relate to the activities of a project coordinator or involve
the construction phase of the project. You will need to determine as to each decision
whether the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
financial interest.

FACTS

You serve without compensation as a Board member and President of the District. The
District has been awarded a grant from the California State Water Resources Control Board through
the State Revolving Fund to fund a water planning project (the "Project") in Alleghany County. The
District will utilize the funds to maintain and improve water sources and is considering two options.
The first option (the "Original Plan") would be to rehabilitate the Ram Spring. The County uses the
spring as its current water source. Currently the water must undergo treatment before it can be used.
The water treatment plant and water tank are so old and in such poor condition that they must be
replaced. The District has adopted a plan for rehabilitating the spring so that it can be reclassified as
ground water. This would eliminate the need for a treatment plant and water tank and would greatly
reduce water production costs. Under the Original Plan, the reclassified Ram Spring water would
continue to be the District's primary water source.

The second option (the "Proposed Plan") would use groundwater to replace the use of the
Ram Spring. This would eliminate the need for a water treatment plant and water tank and would
also accomplish the Project more quickly than the Original Plan. After several consultations and on-
site visits by a representative of the State Revolving Fund's funding division, the representative
recommended that the District adopt the Proposed Plan. Under the Proposed Plan, the District
would still pursue the reclassification of the Ram Spring as groundwater to be used solely as a
backup source, when needed. The Proposed Plan would also include bringing another spring, the
Cumberland Spring, online as an alternate source. Under this plan, all of the run-off in the area of
the Ram Spring could be recaptured.

Test drilling will soon commence and the District Board's decision will be determined by
the results of the test wells. If the tests show that well water does not meet applicable water quality
standards or is insufficient to meet the District's needs, the Board will have no choice but to
approve the Original Plan. If the test wells show that well water can meet water quality standards
and the production needs of the town, the Board will adopt the Proposed Plan.

The Corporation owns the property on which the Ram Spring is located which includes a
gold mine. The District leases that part of the property on which the Ram Spring is located pursuant
to a 99-year lease that terminates in 2076. Under the terms of the lease, the corporation is entitled to
any excess water-flow from the Ram Spring. The District has paid the entire rent for the term of the
lease. You state that the District has no intention of altering or abandoning the lease. The milling of

File No. A-16-060
Page No. 3

gold on the property ceased in1998 but exploration activities continue. You state that both plans
would produce additional water run-off to the mine property.

You state that you believe the water currently available to the mine is adequate, in part,
because the mill used in the mining operation has not been run for many years and, moreover, even
when the mine was running and the District was using the well, there had never been a problem
with not enough water for the mine. Rather than insufficient water, the mine has a problem with
excess water. The mine runs pump between one and four days a week to keep lower levels of the
mine dry. There is a possibility that pumping costs might decrease if the Original Plan is pursued
but that assumes that seepage around the spring area would seep into the mine. You believe,
however, that this speculative.

You state there is no way of knowing, short of conducting an extensive study, what the
difference would be, if any, in the amount of increased water-flow between the two plans. The
Original Plan would increase the water-flow by more efficiently capturing all of the run-off in that
area. The Proposed Plan would increase water-flow as a result of the town's diminished use of the
Ram Spring.

You state that you are unaware of any other effects the project would have under either plan,
on the Corporation or its mining operations.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.)

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material.

Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Those
interests implicated by your account of the facts are the following:

•  A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more
(Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

•  Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

As the Corporation's Secretary you have an interest in the Corporation as both a business
entity that employs you and as a source of income to you.

Foreseeability

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability

File No. A-16-060
Page No. 4

differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Under your facts, the
Corporation is not explicitly involved in the decision to select a plan. The applicable foreseeability
standard is found in Regulation 18701(b), which provides: "In general, if the financial effect can be
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable."

Regulation 18701(b) lists factors to consider in determining whether a governmental
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the official's interest.

(1) The extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent upon intervening
events, not including future governmental decisions by the official's agency, or any other
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the official's agency.

(2) Whether the public official should anticipate a financial effect on his or her financial
interest as a potential outcome under normal circumstances when using appropriate due
diligence and care.

(3) Whether the public official has a financial interest that is of the type that would typically
be affected by the terms of the governmental decision or whether the governmental decision
is of the type that would be expected to have a financial effect on businesses and individuals
similarly situated to those businesses and individuals in which the public official has a
financial interest.

(4) Whether a reasonable inference can be made that the financial effects of the
governmental decision on the public official's financial interest might compromise a public
official's ability to act in a manner consistent with his or her duty to act in the best interests
of the public.

(5) Whether the governmental decision will provide or deny an opportunity, or create an
advantage or disadvantage for one of the official's financial interests, including whether the
financial interest may be entitled to compete or be eligible for a benefit resulting from the
decision.

(6) Whether the public official has the type of financial interest that would cause a similarly
situated person to weigh the advantages and disadvantages of the governmental decision on
his or her financial interest in formulating a position.

Materiality

As applied to your interest in the Corporation as a business entity and as a source of income,
the financial effect of the decision is material if a "prudent person with sufficient information would
find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in
the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business
entity." (Regulation 18702.1).

File No. A-16-060
Page No. 5

Question 1: May you take part in a decision to reinstate the Original Plan?

You state that under either plan, the amount of run-off flowing to the mine property would increase. If wells are used, the increase would result from the District's discontinued use of the Ram Spring as its primary water source. If the District rehabilitates Ram Spring and continues to use it as its primary water source, the increase would result from the increased efficiency in capturing all run-off. You believe that the only possible effect on the Corporation of increased water flow would be increased pumping costs during times when there is too much water in the mine. The amount of excess would depend on weather conditions which vary during the seasons of the year and over longer periods such as periods of drought. You state that you are not aware of any other effects the project, under either plan, would have on the Corporation or its mining operations.

In determining whether the effects of either plan are reasonably foreseeable, we consider the factor set forth in Regulation 18701(b)(2) as the most applicable to the facts here – whether the public official should anticipate a financial effect on his or her financial interest as a potential outcome under normal circumstances when using appropriate due diligence and care.

You state that there is no way of knowing, short of conducting an extensive study, what the increased water flow would be under each of the plans and that any attempt to estimate the amount would be speculative. Therefore, even if you were to conclude that the increased water flow would be detrimental to the mine property, there is no basis for comparing the water flow between the two plans and this would not be a factor in the Board's selection of one plan over the other. Under these facts, the financial effect on the Corporation or its mining operation are hypothetical or theoretical. Therefore, it does not appear to be reasonably foreseeable that the decision to select one plan over the other will materially affect your financial interest.

Questions 2 & 3: May you act as the primary coordinator with engineers working on the project? May you participate in decisions involving the construction phase of the project after the planning phase has been completed?

You have not identified any governmental decisions that may come before the District's Board that would relate to the activities of a project coordinator or involve the construction phase of the project which might trigger Section 87100. Therefore, we are unable to make a determination regarding these decisions. You will need to determine as to each governmental decision whether the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Corporation. You may wish to seek advice as to these decisions as they come before the Board and there are sufficient facts for analyzing whether you have a conflict of interest.

File No. A-16-060
Page No. 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl
